teaching.text: Every plant cell has a nucleus — the control center holding the cell's instructions (DNA). It decides what the cell does, like a tiny brain office.
Two power players: chloroplasts capture sunlight to MAKE food (photosynthesis), and mitochondria RELEASE the energy stored in that food. Maker and releaser — a perfect team.

items.prompt: The nucleus is the cell's boss. Draw a line from the nucleus to its job.
items.explanation: The nucleus holds the DNA instructions and controls everything the cell does.
hints: Think of the part that gives orders — which job sounds like giving orders?
It is not about food, energy or storage. Look for the bossy job.

items.prompt: Chloroplasts are the cell's green kitchens. Connect the chloroplast to its job.
items.explanation: Chloroplasts use sunlight, water and CO₂ to make food — that's photosynthesis, and it's why plants are green.
hints: Green kitchens cook with light. Which job mentions their main ingredient?
The answer involves sunlight going IN — not energy coming out.

items.prompt: Mitochondria are the cell's power stations. Draw their connection.
items.explanation: Mitochondria break food down to set its energy free — that is how the cell gets its power.
hints: A power station doesn't make fuel — it BURNS fuel to get something out.
The chloroplast already took the sunlight job. What's left for the power station?

items.prompt: Food is made, then its energy is released. Connect BOTH the maker and the releaser to their jobs.
items.explanation: Chloroplasts make the food (photosynthesis); mitochondria release its energy (respiration). A two-step energy pipeline.
hints: One organelle is the kitchen, the other is the power station — connect each to its own job.
Sunlight goes to the green one; the energy-release job goes to the other one.

teaching.text: The cell wall is a stiff outer layer around plant cells. It gives the cell its box-like shape and keeps the plant standing tall — animal cells don't have one.
The large central vacuole is the cell's water tank: it stores water and sap, and when it is full, the cell stays plump and the plant stays firm — not wilted.

items.prompt: The cell wall keeps the plant standing. Connect it to its job.
items.explanation: The stiff cell wall supports and protects the cell — it's why plant stems can stand up without bones.
hints: Walls in a building do this same job.
It is not about storing or making anything — think strength.

items.prompt: The vacuole is the cell's storage tank. Draw its connection.
items.explanation: The big central vacuole stores water and sap. A full vacuole keeps the plant firm; an empty one means wilting.
hints: Think of a water tank — what would its job description say?
When this organelle is empty, the plant droops. Which job matches that?

items.prompt: Quick review from last level: reconnect the cell's control center.
items.explanation: The nucleus is still the boss — it holds the DNA and directs the whole cell.
hints: We met this organelle in the previous level — the one with the instructions.
DNA lives inside it. Which job needs the instruction book?

items.prompt: Strong outside, stocked inside: connect BOTH the cell wall and the vacuole to their jobs.
items.explanation: The wall is armor, the vacuole is the tank: support outside, storage inside — together they keep a plant upright and fresh.
hints: One job is about strength, one is about keeping things for later — match each.
The OUTER part gets the support job; the INNER tank gets the other one.

items.prompt: Careful — two jobs on this board belong to NO cell part. Connect only the vacuole, correctly.
items.explanation: Pumping blood and hearing are jobs for organs, not organelles. The vacuole's real job is storing water and sap.
hints: Cells don't pump blood or hear — those are whole-organ jobs. Ignore them.
Stick to the storage tank's real job from the teach card.
